content.text: Tag Enforcement Effects: Deny vs Modify vs Audit

Azure Policy provides three effects relevant to tag enforcement: Deny (block resource creation if the tag is missing or has a wrong value), Modify (automatically add or correct the tag during resource creation or update), and Audit (allow the operation but flag the resource NonCompliant). Each effect serves a different purpose in the tag enforcement lifecycle.

attrs.alt: Tag enforcement effects comparison — Deny, Modify, Audit behavior and setup

content.text: Deny and Modify complement each other: Deny prevents new resources without the tag; Modify automatically populates the tag value when it can be derived from context (resource group tags, subscription tags). Use both together for the strongest enforcement: Modify as the primary mechanism, Deny as the backstop for cases where context is unavailable.

Azure provides a catalog of built-in tag policies that cover the most common tagging requirements. Using built-in policies is faster than authoring custom definitions and ensures you get Microsoft-maintained updates when ARM aliases or policy syntax changes.
Require a tag and its value on resources
Inherit a tag from the resource group
Inherit a tag from the resource group if missing
Inherit a tag from the subscription
Inherit a tag from the subscription if missing
Add or replace a tag on resources
Add or replace a tag on resource groups

# Assign built-in Deny policy — Require CostCenter tag
az policy assignment create \
  --name require-costcenter-tag \
  --policy '871b6d14-10aa-478d-b590-94f262ecfa99' \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "CostCenter"}}' \
  --display-name 'Require CostCenter tag on all resources'

# Assign built-in Modify policy — Inherit CostCenter from resource group
az policy assignment create \
  --name inherit-costcenter-from-rg \
  --policy 'cd3aa116-8754-49c9-a813-ad46512ece54' \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "CostCenter"}}' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus2 \
  --display-name 'Inherit CostCenter from resource group'

# Grant the Modify assignment's managed identity Contributor role
MI_OID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name inherit-costcenter-from-rg \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --assignee $MI_OID \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp

# Create a remediation task to tag existing non-compliant resources
sleep 120  # wait for role propagation
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-inherit-costcenter \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/inherit-costcenter-from-rg \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant

# Monitor remediation
az policy remediation show \
  --name remediate-inherit-costcenter \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query '{Status:provisioningState,Total:deploymentSummary.totalDeployments,Success:deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments,Failed:deploymentSummary.failedDeployments}'

When built-in policies do not fit — for example, you need to enforce a tag value that comes from a list of allowed values rather than a single string — a custom policy is needed. A custom Deny policy with the allowedValues list is more flexible than chaining multiple built-in policies and produces a single NonCompliant result instead of multiple overlapping ones.

Some resources cannot be tagged or should not be tagged — resource types that do not support tags (some classic resources and extension resources), shared infrastructure in a platform subscription, or Microsoft-managed resources. Use notScopes on the tag policy assignment to exclude these scopes rather than creating per-resource exemptions, which do not scale.
Custom Deny Policy: Restricting Tag Values

The built-in 'Require tag with value' policy only ensures a tag is present with an exact value. To restrict the tag to a set of allowed values (for example, only prod, staging, dev, test, or sandbox for the Environment tag), you must author a custom policy with an allowedValues parameter and a notIn field condition. This pattern prevents typos like 'production' or 'Development' from bypassing your cost allocation and compliance automation.

The concat() function builds the alias path dynamically from the parameter. The notIn operator checks whether the field value is NOT in the provided array. Note that the exists check prevents a false positive Deny when the tag is simply absent — when the tag is absent, the 'allowed values' check is irrelevant and the tag presence policy handles it separately.

# Assign the custom allowed-values policy for Environment
POLICY_ID=$(az policy definition show   --name "require-tag-allowed-values"   --management-group mg-corp   --query id -o tsv)

az policy assignment create   --name "env-tag-allowed-values"   --display-name "Environment tag must be an approved value"   --policy "$POLICY_ID"   --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"   --params '{
    "tagName": { "value": "Environment" },
    "allowedValues": { "value": ["prod", "staging", "dev", "test", "sandbox"] }
  }'

# Test: attempt to create a resource group with an invalid Environment value
# This should be blocked by the Deny policy
az group create   --name rg-test-badenv   --location eastus   --tags CostCenter=platform Environment=production Owner=platform-team Application=test
# Expected: RequestDisallowedByPolicy error

# Test: create with valid value
az group create   --name rg-test-goodenv   --location eastus   --tags CostCenter=platform Environment=dev Owner=platform-team Application=test
# Expected: success
Modify Policy: Auto-Tagging from Resource Group

A Modify policy that copies a tag value from the parent resource group to every resource within it is one of the most operationally useful patterns in Azure Policy. Teams set the tag once on the resource group (where it is easy to control) and the Modify policy propagates it to every resource automatically — eliminating the need to pass tag values through every Terraform module or CI/CD pipeline variable.

The condition `notEquals [resourceGroup().tags[tagName]]` fires on both new resources (tag absent) and existing resources where the value has drifted from the resource group's value — for example after a project rename updated the RG tag but not the 200 resources within it. Remediation tasks backfill all drifted resources automatically.

# Create one Modify assignment per tag that should propagate from RG to resource
# Assign at management group level so all subscriptions are covered

PROPAGATE_TAGS=(CostCenter Environment Owner Application)

for TAG in ${PROPAGATE_TAGS[@]}; do
  az policy assignment create     --name "propagate-tag-${TAG,,}"     --display-name "Propagate ${TAG} from resource group"     --policy "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/cd3aa116-8754-49c9-a813-ad46512ece54"     --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"     --mi-system-assigned     --location eastus     --params "{
      "tagName": { "value": "${TAG}" }
    }"
done

# Grant Contributor role to each managed identity
# (done automatically for built-in policy IDs when --mi-system-assigned is used)
# For custom policy assignments, grant explicitly:
for TAG in ${PROPAGATE_TAGS[@]}; do
  ASSIGNMENT_NAME="propagate-tag-${TAG,,}"
  MI_PRINCIPAL=$(az policy assignment show     --name $ASSIGNMENT_NAME     --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"     --query identity.principalId -o tsv)

  az role assignment create     --assignee $MI_PRINCIPAL     --role Contributor     --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"
done

# Create remediation tasks to fix existing non-compliant resources
for TAG in ${PROPAGATE_TAGS[@]}; do
  ASSIGNMENT_ID=$(az policy assignment show     --name "propagate-tag-${TAG,,}"     --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"     --query id -o tsv)

  az policy remediation create     --name "remediate-propagate-${TAG,,}"     --policy-assignment $ASSIGNMENT_ID     --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"     --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance
done
notScopes: Excluding Subscriptions and Resource Groups

The notScopes property on a policy assignment excludes specific child scopes from evaluation. Use it for subscriptions that genuinely cannot comply (a legacy subscription being migrated, a sandbox subscription with relaxed governance, a third-party managed service that owns its own tags). The exclusion is baked into the assignment — it requires no exemption object and does not appear in the exemptions compliance count.

# Add notScopes to an existing assignment using az policy assignment update
ASSIGNMENT_ID=$(az policy assignment show   --name "require-costtag"   --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"   --query id -o tsv)

# Fetch current notScopes and append
CURRENT_SCOPES=$(az policy assignment show --id $ASSIGNMENT_ID --query notScopes -o json)

az policy assignment update   --id $ASSIGNMENT_ID   --set "notScopes=[
    "/subscriptions/aaaaaaaa-0000-1111-2222-bbbbbbbbbbbb",
    "/subscriptions/cccccccc-3333-4444-5555-dddddddddddd/resourceGroups/rg-legacy-migration"
  ]"

# IMPORTANT: notScopes silently excludes resources from compliance counts
# Use exemptions (Waiver/Mitigated) when you need an audit trail
# Use notScopes only for permanently excluded structural carve-outs

# Audit what is excluded: list all assignments and their notScopes
az policy assignment list   --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"   --query '[].{Name:name, NotScopes:notScopes}'   -o table
Policy Initiative: Bundling All Tag Requirements

Rather than assigning a dozen individual tag policies, bundle them into a single policy initiative (policy set definition). The initiative appears as one assignment in the compliance dashboard, provides a single compliance percentage, and makes parameter management straightforward. Teams see one clear mandate — 'tag compliance initiative' — rather than dozens of overlapping assignments with confusing names.

Bicep is well suited for deploying an entire tag governance stack — policy definitions, initiative, assignments, and role assignments — as a repeatable template. Managing these resources as code means the governance configuration is version-controlled alongside the infrastructure it governs, and changes can be reviewed in pull requests before taking effect.

Deny and Audit policies only affect resources created after the policy takes effect. Existing resources that predate the assignment remain non-compliant until a remediation task runs against them. For Modify policies, the remediation task updates each non-compliant resource's tags using the managed identity. For Deny policies there is no remediation — instead run a bulk tagging script to fix existing resources and then the policy enforces the tag for future resources.

# Monitor remediation task status
az policy remediation show   --name "remediate-propagate-costtag"   --management-group mg-corp   --query '{
    status: provisioningState,
    succeeded: resourceCounts.compliant,
    failed: resourceCounts.error,
    remaining: resourceCounts.nonCompliant
  }' -o json

# List all remediation tasks across management group
az policy remediation list   --management-group mg-corp   --query '[].{
    Name: name,
    Assignment: policyAssignmentId,
    State: provisioningState,
    Compliant: resourceCounts.compliant,
    Error: resourceCounts.error
  }' -o table

# Cancel a stuck remediation and restart it
az policy remediation cancel   --name "remediate-propagate-costtag"   --management-group mg-corp

az policy remediation create   --name "remediate-propagate-costtag-v2"   --policy-assignment $(az policy assignment show     --name "modify-propagate-costtag"     --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"     --query id -o tsv)   --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"   --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance

# Scope remediation to a specific subscription (faster for testing)
az policy remediation create   --name "remediate-sub-costtag"   --policy-assignment $(az policy assignment show     --name "modify-propagate-costtag"     --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp"     --query id -o tsv)   --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)"

# Wait for remediation completion (poll every 30s)
while true; do
  STATUS=$(az policy remediation show     --name "remediate-sub-costtag"     --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)"     --query provisioningState -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  echo "$(date -u +%H:%M:%S) Status: $STATUS"
  if [[ "$STATUS" == "Succeeded" || "$STATUS" == "Failed" || "$STATUS" == "Canceled" ]]; then
    break
  fi
  sleep 30
done
Prevent tag-less resources at deploy time

Tag enforcement uses three Azure Policy effects: Deny (block create/update if tag is missing or wrong), Modify (automatically add or correct the tag using a managed identity), and Audit (allow but flag NonCompliant for dashboard tracking). Built-in tag policies cover the common cases — require a tag (Deny), inherit from resource group or subscription (Modify), and add or replace (Modify). Custom policies are needed for allowedValues enforcement or multi-tag requirements. The Modify effect requires a system-assigned managed identity with Contributor on the assignment scope; use a remediation task to apply the Modify policy to existing resources. Use notScopes on the assignment to exclude subscriptions or resource group paths that should not be covered.
